infobox.value: | **Succeeded by** | | |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire") | |  | [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") | |  | [Sikh Empire](/wiki/Sikh_Empire "Sikh Empire") | |  | [Ahom kingdom](/wiki/Ahom_kingdom "Ahom kingdom") | |  | [Bengal Subah](/wiki/Bengal_Subah "Bengal Subah") | |  | [Oudh State](/wiki/Oudh_State "Oudh State") | |  | [Carnatic Sultanate](/wiki/Carnatic_Sultanate "Carnatic Sultanate") | |  | [Kingdom of Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore") | |  | [*See list of other states*](/wiki/Doctrine_of_lapse#Princely_states_annexed_under_the_doctrine "Doctrine of lapse") | | |  |  | | --- | --- | | [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") |  | | | |

(sometimes also **Company Raj**,[[7]](#cite_note-7) from [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") *rāj*, lit. 'rule'[[8]](#cite_note-8)) was the rule of the [British East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") on the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). This is variously taken to have commenced in 1757, after the [Battle of Plassey](/wiki/Battle_of_Plassey "Battle of Plassey"), when the [Nawab of Bengal](/wiki/Nawab_of_Bengal "Nawab of Bengal") [Siraj ud-Daulah](/wiki/Siraj_ud-Daulah "Siraj ud-Daulah") was defeated and replaced with [Mir Jafar](/wiki/Mir_Jafar "Mir Jafar"), who had the support of the East India Company;[[9]](#cite_note-9) or in 1765, when the Company was granted the [*diwani*](/wiki/Dewan#Diwani_in_British_India "Dewan"), or the right to collect revenue, in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") and [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar");[[10]](#cite_note-10) or in 1773, when the Company abolished local rule (Nizamat) in Bengal and established a capital in [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"), appointed its first [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India") of [Fort William](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency"), [Warren Hastings](/wiki/Warren_Hastings "Warren Hastings"), and became directly involved in governance.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Period of Indian history (1757–1858)

This article is about the rule of the East India Company on the Indian subcontinent from 1757 to 1858. For rule by the British Crown from 1858 to 1947, see [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj"). For the history of the East India Company until 1756, see [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company").

"Company rule" redirects here. For Rhodesia, see [Company rule in Rhodesia](/wiki/Company_rule_in_Rhodesia "Company rule in Rhodesia"). For the East Indies, see [Company rule in the Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Company_rule_in_the_Dutch_East_Indies "Company rule in the Dutch East Indies").

**Company rule in India** (sometimes also **Company Raj**,[[7]](#cite_note-7) from [Hindi](/wiki/Hindi "Hindi") *rāj*, lit. 'rule'[[8]](#cite_note-8)) was the rule of the [British East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") on the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"). This is variously taken to have commenced in 1757, after the [Battle of Plassey](/wiki/Battle_of_Plassey "Battle of Plassey"), when the [Nawab of Bengal](/wiki/Nawab_of_Bengal "Nawab of Bengal") [Siraj ud-Daulah](/wiki/Siraj_ud-Daulah "Siraj ud-Daulah") was defeated and replaced with [Mir Jafar](/wiki/Mir_Jafar "Mir Jafar"), who had the support of the East India Company;[[9]](#cite_note-9) or in 1765, when the Company was granted the [*diwani*](/wiki/Dewan#Diwani_in_British_India "Dewan"), or the right to collect revenue, in [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") and [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar");[[10]](#cite_note-10) or in 1773, when the Company abolished local rule (Nizamat) in Bengal and established a capital in [Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"), appointed its first [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India") of [Fort William](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency"), [Warren Hastings](/wiki/Warren_Hastings "Warren Hastings"), and became directly involved in governance.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The Company's rule lasted until 1858, when, after the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857") and the [Government of India Act 1858](/wiki/Government_of_India_Act_1858 "Government of India Act 1858"), the [India Office](/wiki/India_Office "India Office") of the [British government](/wiki/British_government "British government") assumed the task of directly administering India in the new [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").

## Expansion and territory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Expansion and territory")]

The [English East India Company](/wiki/English_East_India_Company "English East India Company") ("the Company") was founded in 1600, as *The Company of Merchants of London Trading into the East Indies*. It gained a foothold in India with the establishment of a [factory](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)") in [Masulipatnam](/wiki/Masulipatnam "Masulipatnam") on the Eastern coast of India in 1611 and the grant of the rights to establish a factory in [Surat](/wiki/Surat "Surat") in 1612 by the Mughal Emperor [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir"). In 1640, after receiving similar permission from the [Vijayanagara ruler](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire") further south, a second factory was established in [Madras](/wiki/Madras "Madras") on the southeastern coast. [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay") island, not far from Surat, a former [Portuguese outpost](/wiki/Portuguese_possessions "Portuguese possessions") gifted to [England](/wiki/Kingdom_of_England "Kingdom of England") as [dowry](/wiki/Dowry "Dowry") in the marriage of [Catherine of Braganza](/wiki/Catherine_of_Braganza "Catherine of Braganza") to [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), was leased by the Company in 1668. Following the [Anglo-Mughal War](/wiki/Anglo-Mughal_War_(1686%E2%80%931690) "Anglo-Mughal War (1686–1690)"), the Company was allowed by [Emperor Aurangzeb](/wiki/Emperor_Aurangzeb "Emperor Aurangzeb") to establish a presence on the eastern coast as well; far up that coast, in the [Ganges Delta](/wiki/Ganges_Delta "Ganges Delta"), a factory was set up in Calcutta. Since, during this time other *companies*—established by the [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India"), [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company"), [French](/wiki/Louis_XIV%27s_East_India_Company "Louis XIV's East India Company"), and [Danish](/wiki/Danish_East_India_Company "Danish East India Company")—were similarly expanding in the region, the English Company's unremarkable beginnings on [coastal India](/wiki/Coastal_India "Coastal India") offered no clues to what would become a lengthy presence on the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent").

The company's victory under [Robert Clive](/wiki/Robert_Clive "Robert Clive") in the 1757 Battle of Plassey and another victory in the 1764 [Battle of Buxar](/wiki/Battle_of_Buxar "Battle of Buxar") (in Bihar) consolidated the company's power and forced emperor [Shah Alam II](/wiki/Shah_Alam_II "Shah Alam II") to appoint it the *[diwan](/wiki/Diwan_(title) "Diwan (title)")*, or revenue collector, of [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), Bihar, and [Orissa](/wiki/Odisha "Odisha"). The Company thus became the de facto ruler of large areas of the [lower Gangetic plain](/wiki/Lower_Gangetic_plains_moist_deciduous_forests "Lower Gangetic plains moist deciduous forests") by 1773. It also proceeded by degrees to expand its dominions around Bombay and Madras. The [Anglo-Mysore Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Mysore_Wars "Anglo-Mysore Wars") (1766–1799) and the [Anglo-Maratha Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Maratha_Wars_(disambiguation) "Anglo-Maratha Wars (disambiguation)") (1772–1818) left it in control of large areas of India south of the [Sutlej River](/wiki/Sutlej_River "Sutlej River"). With the defeat of the [Marathas](/wiki/Maratha_Confederacy "Maratha Confederacy"), no native power represented a threat for the Company any longer.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The expansion of the company's power chiefly took two forms. The first of these was the outright annexation of Indian states and subsequent direct governance of the underlying regions, which collectively came to comprise [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India"). The annexed regions included the [North-Western Provinces](/wiki/North-Western_Provinces "North-Western Provinces") (comprising [Rohilkhand](/wiki/Rohilkhand "Rohilkhand"), [Gorakhpur](/wiki/Gorakhpur "Gorakhpur"), and the [Doab](/wiki/Doab "Doab")) (1801), Delhi (1803), Assam ([Ahom Kingdom](/wiki/Ahom_Kingdom "Ahom Kingdom") 1828), and [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") (1843). [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region"), [North-West Frontier Province](/wiki/North-West_Frontier_Province "North-West Frontier Province"), and [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir "Kashmir"), were annexed after the [Anglo-Sikh Wars](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War") in 1849-1856 (Period of tenure of Marquess of Dalhousie Governor General); however, Kashmir was immediately sold under the [Treaty of Amritsar](/wiki/Treaty_of_Amritsar_(1846) "Treaty of Amritsar (1846)") (1850) to the [Dogra dynasty](/wiki/Dogra_dynasty "Dogra dynasty") of [Jammu](/wiki/Jammu "Jammu"), and thereby became a princely state. In 1854 [Berar](/wiki/Berar_Province "Berar Province") was annexed, and the state of [Oudh](/wiki/Awadh "Awadh") two years later.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The second form of asserting power involved treaties in which Indian rulers acknowledged the company's [hegemony](/wiki/Hegemony "Hegemony") in return for limited internal [autonomy](/wiki/Autonomy "Autonomy"). Since the Company operated under financial constraints, it had to set up *political* underpinnings for its rule.[[13]](#cite_note-brown-p67-13) The most important such support came from the *[subsidiary alliances](/wiki/Subsidiary_alliance "Subsidiary alliance")* with Indian princes during the first 75 years of Company rule.[[13]](#cite_note-brown-p67-13) In the early 19th century, the territories of these princes accounted for two-third of India.[[13]](#cite_note-brown-p67-13) When an Indian ruler, who was able to secure his territory, wanted to enter such an alliance, the Company welcomed it as an economical method of indirect rule, which did not involve the economic costs of direct administration or the political costs of gaining the support of alien subjects.[[14]](#cite_note-brown-68-14)

In return, the Company undertook the "defence of these subordinate allies and treated them with traditional respect and marks of honor."[[14]](#cite_note-brown-68-14) Subsidiary alliances created the [princely states](/wiki/Princely_States "Princely States"), of the Hindu [maharajas](/wiki/Maharaja "Maharaja") and the Muslim [nawabs](/wiki/Nawab "Nawab"). Prominent among the princely states were: [Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin") (1791), [Jaipur](/wiki/Jaipur_State "Jaipur State") (1794), [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") (1795), [Hyderabad](/wiki/Hyderabad_State "Hyderabad State") (1798), [Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore") (1799), [Cis-Sutlej Hill States](/wiki/Cis-Sutlej_states "Cis-Sutlej states") (1815), [Central India Agency](/wiki/Central_India_Agency "Central India Agency") (1819), [Cutch](/wiki/Cutch_State "Cutch State") and [Gujarat Gaikwad territories](/wiki/Baroda_State "Baroda State") (1807–1820), [Rajputana](/wiki/Rajputana "Rajputana") (1818),[[15]](#cite_note-ludden-expansion-15) and [Bahawalpur](/wiki/Bahawalpur_(princely_state) "Bahawalpur (princely state)") (1833).

## Regulation of Company rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Regulation of Company rule")]

Until Clive's victory at [Plassey](/wiki/Battle_of_Plassey "Battle of Plassey"), the East India Company territories in India, which consisted largely of the [presidency](/wiki/Presidency "Presidency") towns of Calcutta, Madras, and Bombay, were governed by the mostly autonomous—and sporadically unmanageable—*town councils*, all composed of merchants.[[18]](#cite_note-igi-bandyo-post-clive-18) The councils barely had enough powers for the effective management of their local affairs, and the ensuing lack of oversight of the overall Company operations in India led to some grave abuses by Company officers or their allies.[[18]](#cite_note-igi-bandyo-post-clive-18) Clive's victory, and the award of the *diwani* of the rich region of Bengal, brought India into the public spotlight in Britain.[[18]](#cite_note-igi-bandyo-post-clive-18) The company's money management practices came to be questioned, especially as it began to post net losses even as some Company servants, the "Nabobs", returned to Britain with large fortunes, which—according to rumours then current—were acquired unscrupulously.[[19]](#cite_note-19) By 1772, the Company needed British government loans to stay afloat, and there was fear in London that the company's corrupt practices could soon seep into British business and public life.[[20]](#cite_note-Peers_2006_35-20) The rights and duties of the British government with regards the company's new territories also came to be examined.[[21]](#cite_note-marshall-207-21) The British parliament then held several inquiries and in 1773, during the premiership of [Lord North](/wiki/Frederick_North,_Lord_North "Frederick North, Lord North"), enacted the [Regulating Act 1773](/wiki/Regulating_Act_1773 "Regulating Act 1773"), which established regulations, its long title stated, "for the better Management of the Affairs of the *East India Company*, as well in *India* as in *Europe*".[[22]](#cite_note-igi-14-22)

Although [Lord North](/wiki/Lord_North "Lord North") himself wanted the company's territories to be taken over by the British state,[[21]](#cite_note-marshall-207-21) he faced determined political opposition from many quarters, including some in the [City of London](/wiki/City_of_London "City of London") and the [Parliament of Great Britain](/wiki/Parliament_of_Great_Britain "Parliament of Great Britain").[[20]](#cite_note-Peers_2006_35-20) The result was a compromise in which the Regulating Act—although implying the ultimate sovereignty of the [British Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") over these new territories—asserted that the company could act as a sovereign power on behalf of the Crown.[[23]](#cite_note-marshall-197-23) It could do this while concurrently being subject to oversight and regulation by the British government and parliament.[[23]](#cite_note-marshall-197-23) The Court of Directors of the company were required under the act to submit all communications regarding civil, military, and revenue matters in India for scrutiny by the British government.[[24]](#cite_note-bandyo-77-24) For the governance of the Indian territories, the act asserted the supremacy of the [Presidency of Fort William (Bengal)](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") over those of [Fort St. George (Madras)](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency") and [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency").[[25]](#cite_note-igi-iv-p14-25) It also nominated a Governor-General (Warren Hastings) and four councillors for administering the Bengal Presidency (and for overseeing the company's operations in India).[[25]](#cite_note-igi-iv-p14-25) "The subordinate Presidencies were forbidden to wage war or make treaties without the previous consent of the Governor-General of Bengal in Council,[[26]](#cite_note-26) except in case of imminent necessity. The Governors of these Presidencies were directed in general terms to obey the orders of the Governor-General-in-Council, and to transmit to him intelligence of all important matters."[[22]](#cite_note-igi-14-22) However, the imprecise wording of the Act left it open to be variously interpreted; consequently, the administration in India continued to be hobbled by disunity between the provincial governors, between members of the council, and between the Governor-General himself and his Council.[[24]](#cite_note-bandyo-77-24) The *Regulating Act* also attempted to address the prevalent corruption in India: Company servants were henceforth forbidden to engage in private trade in India or to receive "presents" from Indian nationals.[[22]](#cite_note-igi-14-22)

In 1783, the [Fox–North coalition](/wiki/Fox%E2%80%93North_coalition "Fox–North coalition") tried to reform colonial policy again with a bill introduced by [Edmund Burke](/wiki/Edmund_Burke "Edmund Burke") which would have transferred political power over India from the East India Company to a parliamentary commission. The bill passed the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Great_Britain "House of Commons of Great Britain") with the enthusiastic support of Foreign Secretary [Charles James Fox](/wiki/Charles_James_Fox "Charles James Fox"), but was vetoed by the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords") under pressure from King [George III](/wiki/George_III "George III"), who then dismissed the government and formed a [new ministry](/wiki/First_Pitt_ministry "First Pitt ministry") under Fox's rival [William Pitt the Younger](/wiki/William_Pitt_the_Younger "William Pitt the Younger"). [Pitt's India Act](/wiki/Pitt%27s_India_Act "Pitt's India Act") left the East India Company in political control of India but established a [Board of Control](/wiki/India_Board "India Board") in England both to supervise the East India Company's affairs and to prevent the company's shareholders from interfering in the governance of India.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The Board of Control consisted of six members, which included one Secretary of State from the British cabinet, as well as the [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer").[[24]](#cite_note-bandyo-77-24) Around this time, there was also extensive debate in the British Parliament on the issue of landed rights in Bengal, with a consensus developing in support of the view advocated by [Philip Francis](/wiki/Philip_Francis_(English_politician) "Philip Francis (English politician)"), a member of the Bengal council and political adversary of Warren Hastings, that all lands in Bengal should be considered the "estate and inheritance of native land-holders and families".[[29]](#cite_note-travers-213-29)

Mindful of the reports of abuse and corruption in Bengal by Company servants, the India Act itself noted numerous complaints that "'divers Rajahs, Zamindars, Talukdars, and landholders' had been unjustly deprived of 'their lands, jurisdictions, rights, and privileges'".[[29]](#cite_note-travers-213-29) At the same time the company's directors were now leaning towards Francis's view that the land-tax in Bengal should be made fixed and permanent, setting the stage for the [Permanent Settlement](/wiki/Permanent_Settlement "Permanent Settlement") (see section [Revenue collection](#Revenue_collection) below).[[30]](#cite_note-30) The India Act also created in each of the three presidencies a number of administrative and military posts, which included: a Governor and three Councilors, one of which was the Commander in Chief of the Presidency army.[[31]](#cite_note-bandyo-78-31) Although the supervisory powers of the Governor-General-in-Council in Bengal (over Madras and Bombay) were extended—as they were again in the [Charter Act 1793](/wiki/Charter_Act_1793 "Charter Act 1793")—the subordinate presidencies continued to exercise some autonomy until both the extension of British territories into becoming contiguous and the advent of faster communications in the next century.[[32]](#cite_note-igi-15-32)

Still, the new Governor-General appointed in 1786, [Lord Cornwallis](/wiki/Charles_Cornwallis,_1st_Marquess_Cornwallis "Charles Cornwallis, 1st Marquess Cornwallis"), not only had more power than Hastings, but also had the support of a powerful British cabinet minister, [Henry Dundas](/wiki/Henry_Dundas "Henry Dundas"), who, as Secretary of State for the [Home Office](/wiki/Home_Office "Home Office"), was in charge of the overall India policy.[[33]](#cite_note-33) From 1784 onwards, the British government had the final word on all major appointments in India; a candidate's suitability for a senior position was often decided by the strength of his political connections rather than that of his administrative ability.[[34]](#cite_note-peers-36-34) Although this practice resulted in many Governor-General nominees being chosen from Britain's conservative [landed gentry](/wiki/Landed_gentry "Landed gentry"), there were some liberals as well, such as [Lord William Bentinck](/wiki/Lord_William_Bentinck "Lord William Bentinck") and [Lord Dalhousie](/wiki/George_Ramsay,_9th_Earl_of_Dalhousie "George Ramsay, 9th Earl of Dalhousie").[[34]](#cite_note-peers-36-34)

British political opinion was also shaped by the attempted [Impeachment of Warren Hastings](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Warren_Hastings "Impeachment of Warren Hastings"); the trial, whose proceedings began in 1788, ended with Hastings' acquittal, in 1795.[[35]](#cite_note-peers-36-37-35) Although the effort was chiefly coordinated by Edmund Burke, it also drew support from within the British government.[[35]](#cite_note-peers-36-37-35) Burke accused Hastings not only of corruption, but—appealing to universal standards of justice—also of acting solely upon his own discretion, without concern for law, and of wilfully causing distress to others in India. Hastings' defenders countered that his actions were consistent with Indian customs and traditions.[[35]](#cite_note-peers-36-37-35) Although Burke's speeches at the trial drew applause and focused attention on India, Hastings was eventually acquitted, due in part to the revival of [nationalism in Britain](/wiki/British_nationalism "British nationalism") in the wake of the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"). Nonetheless, Burke's effort had the effect of creating a sense of responsibility in British public life for the company's dominion in India.[[35]](#cite_note-peers-36-37-35)

Soon rumblings appeared amongst merchants in London that the monopoly granted to the East India Company in 1600, intended to facilitate its competition against [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Empire "Dutch Empire") and [French](/wiki/French_colonial_empire "French colonial empire") in a distant region, was no longer needed.[[32]](#cite_note-igi-15-32) In response, in the [Charter Act 1813](/wiki/Charter_Act_1813 "Charter Act 1813"), the British Parliament renewed the company's charter but terminated its monopoly except with regard to tea and trade with China, opening India both to private investment and missionaries.[[36]](#cite_note-ludden-134-36) With increased British power in India, supervision of Indian affairs by the [British Crown](/wiki/British_Crown "British Crown") and [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") increased as well. By the 1820s British nationals could transact business or engage in missionary work under the protection of the Crown in the three presidencies.[[36]](#cite_note-ludden-134-36) Finally, under the terms of the [Saint Helena Act 1833](/wiki/Saint_Helena_Act_1833 "Saint Helena Act 1833"), the British Parliament revoked the company's monopoly in the China trade and made it an agent for the administration of British India.[[36]](#cite_note-ludden-134-36) The [Governor-General of Bengal](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Bengal "Governor-General of Bengal") was redesignated as the [Governor-General of India](/wiki/Governor-General_of_India "Governor-General of India"). The Governor-General and his executive council were given exclusive legislative powers for the whole of British India.[[32]](#cite_note-igi-15-32) Since the British territories in north India had now extended up to Delhi, the act also sanctioned the creation of a [Presidency of Agra](/wiki/Presidency_of_Agra "Presidency of Agra").[[32]](#cite_note-igi-15-32) With the annexation of [Oudh](/wiki/Awadh "Awadh") in 1856, this territory was extended and eventually became the [United Provinces of Agra and Oudh](/wiki/United_Provinces_of_Agra_and_Oudh "United Provinces of Agra and Oudh").[[32]](#cite_note-igi-15-32) In addition, in 1854, a lieutenant-governor was appointed for the region of Bengal, Bihar and Odisha, leaving the Governor-General to concentrate on the governance of India as a whole.[[32]](#cite_note-igi-15-32)

## Revenue collection

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Revenue collection")]

Main articles: [Permanent Settlement](/wiki/Permanent_Settlement "Permanent Settlement"), [Zamindar](/wiki/Zamindar "Zamindar"), [Ryotwari](/wiki/Ryotwari "Ryotwari"), and [Economy of India under Company rule](/wiki/Economy_of_India_under_Company_rule "Economy of India under Company rule")

In the remnant of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") revenue system existing in pre-1765 Bengal, [zamindars](/wiki/Zamindar "Zamindar"), or "land holders", collected revenue on behalf of the Mughal emperor, whose representative, or *[diwan](/wiki/Diwan_(title) "Diwan (title)")*, supervised their activities.[[37]](#cite_note-37) In this system, the assortment of rights associated with land were not possessed by a "land owner", but rather shared by the several parties with stake in the land, including the peasant cultivator, the *zamindar*, and the state.[[38]](#cite_note-metcalf-78-38) The *zamindar* served as an intermediary who procured rent from the cultivator, and after withholding a percentage for his own expenses, made available the rest, as [revenue](/wiki/Revenue "Revenue") to the state.[[38]](#cite_note-metcalf-78-38) Under the Mughal system, the land itself belonged to the state and not to the *zamindar*, who could transfer only his right to collect rent.[[38]](#cite_note-metcalf-78-38) On being awarded the *diwani* or overlordship of Bengal following the [Battle of Buxar](/wiki/Battle_of_Buxar "Battle of Buxar") in 1764, the [East India Company](/wiki/Honourable_East_India_Company "Honourable East India Company") found itself short of trained administrators, especially those familiar with local custom and law; tax collection was consequently farmed out. This uncertain foray into land taxation by the company, may have gravely worsened the impact of a [famine that struck Bengal in 1769–70](/wiki/Bengal_famine_of_1770 "Bengal famine of 1770"), in which between seven and ten million people—or between a quarter and third of the presidency's population—may have died.[[39]](#cite_note-peers-metcalf-famine-39) However, the company provided little relief either through reduced taxation or by relief efforts,[[40]](#cite_note-peers-47-40) and the economic and cultural impact of the famine was felt decades later, even becoming, a century later, the subject of [Bankim Chandra Chatterjee](/wiki/Bankim_Chandra_Chatterjee "Bankim Chandra Chatterjee")'s novel *[Anandamath](/wiki/Anandamath "Anandamath")*.[[39]](#cite_note-peers-metcalf-famine-39)

In 1772, under Warren Hastings, the East India Company took over revenue collection directly in the [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency") (then Bengal and Bihar), establishing a Board of Revenue with offices in Calcutta and [Patna](/wiki/Patna "Patna"), and moving the pre-existing Mughal revenue records from [Murshidabad](/wiki/Murshidabad "Murshidabad") to Calcutta.[[41]](#cite_note-robb-revenue-41)
In 1773, after [Oudh](/wiki/Awadh "Awadh") ceded the tributary state of [Benaras](/wiki/Benaras "Benaras"), the revenue collection system was extended to the territory with a Company [Resident](/wiki/Resident_(title)#Residents_in_(British)_Asia "Resident (title)") in charge.[[41]](#cite_note-robb-revenue-41) The following year—with a view to preventing corruption—Company *district collectors*, who were then responsible for revenue collection for an entire district, were replaced with provincial councils at Patna, Murshidabad, and Calcutta, and with Indian collectors working within each district.[[41]](#cite_note-robb-revenue-41) The title, "collector", reflected "the centrality of land revenue collection to government in India: it was the government's primary function and it moulded the institutions and patterns of administration".[[42]](#cite_note-42)

The Company inherited a revenue collection system from the Mughals in which the heaviest proportion of the tax burden fell on the cultivators, with one-third of the production reserved for imperial entitlement; this pre-colonial system became the Company revenue policy's baseline.[[43]](#cite_note-peers-p45-47-43) However, there was vast variation across India in the methods by which the revenues were collected; with this complication in mind, a Committee of Circuit toured the districts of expanded Bengal Presidency in order to make a five-year settlement, consisting of five-yearly inspections and temporary [tax farming](/wiki/Tax_farming "Tax farming").[[44]](#cite_note-44) In their overall approach to revenue policy, Company officials were guided by two goals: first, preserving as much as possible the balance of rights and obligations that were traditionally claimed by the farmers who cultivated the land and the various intermediaries who collected tax on the state's behalf and who reserved a cut for themselves; and second, identifying those sectors of the rural economy that would maximise both revenue and security.[[43]](#cite_note-peers-p45-47-43) Although their first revenue settlement turned out to be essentially the same as the more informal pre-existing Mughal one, the company had created a foundation for the growth of both information and bureaucracy.[[43]](#cite_note-peers-p45-47-43)

In 1793, the new Governor-General, [Lord Cornwallis](/wiki/Charles_Cornwallis,_1st_Marquess_Cornwallis "Charles Cornwallis, 1st Marquess Cornwallis"), promulgated the permanent settlement of land revenues in the presidency, the first socio-economic regulation in colonial India.[[41]](#cite_note-robb-revenue-41) By the terms of the settlement rajas and taluqdars were recognised as zamindars and they were asked to collect the rent from the peasants and pay revenue to the company. It was named *permanent* because it fixed the land tax in perpetuity in return for landed property rights for [zamindars](/wiki/Zamindars "Zamindars"); it simultaneously defined the nature of land ownership in the presidency, and gave individuals and families separate property rights in occupied land. Since the revenue was fixed in perpetuity, it was fixed at a high level, which in Bengal amounted to £3 million at 1789–90 prices. According to the [Permanent Settlement](/wiki/Permanent_Settlement "Permanent Settlement") if the zamindars failed to pay the revenue on time, the zamindari right would be taken from them.[[45]](#cite_note-45) According to one estimate, this was 20% higher than the revenue demand before 1757.[[46]](#cite_note-46) Over the next century, partly as a result of land surveys, court rulings, and property sales, the change was given practical dimension.[[47]](#cite_note-47) An influence on the development of this revenue policy were the economic theories then current, which regarded agriculture as the engine of economic development, and consequently stressed the fixing of revenue demands in order to encourage growth.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The expectation behind the permanent settlement was that knowledge of a fixed government demand would encourage the zamindars to increase both their average outcrop and the land under cultivation, since they would be able to retain the profits from the increased output; in addition, it was envisaged that land itself would become a marketable form of property that could be purchased, sold, or mortgaged.[[43]](#cite_note-peers-p45-47-43) A feature of this economic rationale was the additional expectation that the zamindars, recognising their own best interest, would not make unreasonable demands on the peasantry.[[49]](#cite_note-bose-ps-49)

However, these expectations were not realised in practice, and in many regions of Bengal, the peasants bore the brunt of the increased demand, there being little protection for their traditional rights in the new legislation.[[49]](#cite_note-bose-ps-49) [Forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour") of the peasants by the zamindars became more prevalent as cash crops were cultivated to meet the Company revenue demands.[[43]](#cite_note-peers-p45-47-43) Although commercialised cultivation was not new to the region, it had now penetrated deeper into village society and made it more vulnerable to market forces.[[43]](#cite_note-peers-p45-47-43) The zamindars themselves were often unable to meet the increased demands that the company had placed on them;[[50]](#cite_note-50) consequently, many defaulted, and by one estimate, up to one-third of their lands were auctioned during the first two decades following the permanent settlement. The new owners were often [Brahmin](/wiki/Brahmin "Brahmin") and [Kayastha](/wiki/Kayastha "Kayastha") employees of the Company who had a good grasp of the new system, and had, in many cases, prospered from it.[[51]](#cite_note-metcalf-revenue-51)

Since the zamindars were never able to undertake costly improvements to the land envisaged under the Permanent Settlement, some of which required the removal of the existing farmers, they soon became rentiers who lived off the rent from their tenant farmers.[[51]](#cite_note-metcalf-revenue-51) In many areas, especially northern Bengal, they had to increasingly share the revenue with intermediate tenure holders, called *jotedars*, who supervised farming in the villages.[[51]](#cite_note-metcalf-revenue-51) Consequently, unlike the contemporaneous [Enclosure movement](/wiki/Enclosure_movement "Enclosure movement") in Britain, agriculture in Bengal remained the province of the subsistence farming of innumerable small [paddy fields](/wiki/Paddy_field "Paddy field").[[51]](#cite_note-metcalf-revenue-51)

The zamindari system was one of two principal revenue settlements undertaken by the Company in India.[[52]](#cite_note-52) In [southern India](/wiki/South_India "South India"), [Thomas Munro](/wiki/Sir_Thomas_Munro,_1st_Baronet "Sir Thomas Munro, 1st Baronet"), who would later become Governor of Madras, promoted the *[ryotwari](/wiki/Ryotwari "Ryotwari")* system or the Munro system, in which the government settled land-revenue directly with the peasant farmers, or *ryots*.[[40]](#cite_note-peers-47-40) It was first tried in small scale by Captain Alexander Read in the areas that were taken over from the wars with Tipu Sultan. Subsequently, developed by Thomas Munro, this system was gradually extended all over South India. This was, in part, a consequence of the turmoil of the [Anglo-Mysore Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Mysore_Wars "Anglo-Mysore Wars"), which had prevented the emergence of a class of large landowners; in addition, Munro and others felt that *ryotwari* was closer to traditional practice in the region and ideologically more progressive, allowing the benefits of Company rule to reach the lowest levels of rural society.[[40]](#cite_note-peers-47-40) At the heart of the *ryotwari* system was a particular theory of [economic rent](/wiki/Economic_rent "Economic rent")—and based on [David Ricardo](/wiki/David_Ricardo "David Ricardo")'s [Law of Rent](/wiki/Law_of_Rent "Law of Rent")—promoted by [utilitarian](/wiki/Utilitarian "Utilitarian") [James Mill](/wiki/James_Mill "James Mill") who formulated the Indian revenue policy between 1819 and 1830. "He believed that the government was the ultimate lord of the soil and should not renounce its right to 'rent', i.e. the profit left over on richer soil when wages and other working expenses had been settled."[[53]](#cite_note-brown-p66-53) Another keystone of the new system of temporary settlements was the classification of agricultural fields according to soil type and produce, with average rent rates fixed for the period of the settlement.[[54]](#cite_note-54) According to Mill, taxation of land rent would promote efficient agriculture and simultaneously prevent the emergence of a "parasitic landlord class".[[53]](#cite_note-brown-p66-53) Mill advocated *ryotwari* settlements which consisted of government measurement and assessment of each plot (valid for 20 or 30 years) and subsequent taxation which was dependent on the fertility of the soil.[[53]](#cite_note-brown-p66-53) The taxed amount was nine-tenths of the "rent" in the early 19th century and gradually fell afterwards.[[53]](#cite_note-brown-p66-53) However, in spite of the appeal of the *ryotwari* system's abstract principles, class hierarchies in southern Indian villages had not entirely disappeared—for example village headmen continued to hold sway—and peasant cultivators sometimes came to experience revenue demands they could not meet.[[55]](#cite_note-55) In the 1850s, a scandal erupted when it was discovered that some Indian revenue agents of the company were using torture to meet the company's revenue demands.[[40]](#cite_note-peers-47-40)

Land revenue settlements constituted a major administrative activity of the various governments in India under Company rule.[[13]](#cite_note-brown-p67-13) In all areas other than the Bengal Presidency, land settlement work involved a continually repetitive process of surveying and measuring plots, assessing their quality, and recording landed rights, and constituted a large proportion of the work of [Indian Civil Service](/wiki/Indian_Civil_Service "Indian Civil Service") officers working for the government.[[13]](#cite_note-brown-p67-13) After the Company lost its trading rights, it became the single most important source of government revenue, roughly half of overall revenue in the middle of the 19th century;[[13]](#cite_note-brown-p67-13) even so, between the years 1814 and 1859, the government of India ran debts in 33 years.[[13]](#cite_note-brown-p67-13) With expanded dominion, even during non-deficit years, there was just enough money to pay the salaries of a threadbare administration, a skeleton police force, and the army.[[13]](#cite_note-brown-p67-13)

## Army and civil service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Army and civil service")]

Main article: [Presidency armies](/wiki/Presidency_armies "Presidency armies")

In 1772, when Hastings became the first Governor-General one of his first undertakings was the rapid expansion of the Presidency's army. Since the available soldiers, or *[Sepoys](/wiki/Sepoy "Sepoy")*, from Bengal—many of whom had fought against the British in the Battle of Plassey – were now suspect in British eyes, Hastings recruited farther west from the "major breeding ground" of India's infantry in eastern [Awadh](/wiki/Awadh "Awadh") and the lands around [Banaras](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi") including [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar").[[56]](#cite_note-bayly-84-86-56) The [high caste](/wiki/Caste_system_in_India "Caste system in India") rural Hindu [Rajputs](/wiki/Rajput "Rajput") and [Brahmins](/wiki/Brahmins "Brahmins") of this region, known as *[Purbiyas](/wiki/Purbiya "Purbiya")* (Hindi, lit. "easterners"), had been recruited by Mughal Empire armies for two hundred years;[[56]](#cite_note-bayly-84-86-56) the East India Company continued this practice for the next 75 years, with these soldiers comprising up to eighty per cent of the Bengal army. However, in order to avoid any friction within the ranks, the company also took pains to adapt its military practices to their religious requirements. Consequently, these soldiers dined in separate facilities; in addition, overseas service, considered polluting to their caste, was not required of them, and the army soon came to recognise Hindu festivals officially. "This encouragement of high caste ritual status, however, left the government vulnerable to protest, even mutiny, whenever the sepoys detected infringement of their prerogatives."[[57]](#cite_note-57)

The [Bengal Army](/wiki/Bengal_Army "Bengal Army") was used in military campaigns in other parts of India and abroad: to provide crucial support to a weak Madras army in the [Third Anglo-Mysore War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Mysore_War "Third Anglo-Mysore War") in 1791, and also in [Java](/wiki/Java "Java") and [Ceylon](/wiki/Ceylon "Ceylon").[[56]](#cite_note-bayly-84-86-56) In contrast to the soldiers in the armies of Indian rulers, the Bengal sepoys not only received high pay, but also received it reliably, thanks in great measure to the company's access to the vast land-revenue reserves of Bengal.[[56]](#cite_note-bayly-84-86-56) Soon, bolstered both by the new musket technology and naval support, the Bengal army came to be widely well-regarded.[[56]](#cite_note-bayly-84-86-56) The well-disciplined sepoys attired in red-coats and their British officers began to arouse "a kind of awe in their adversaries. In Maharashtra and in Java, the sepoys were regarded as the embodiment of demonic forces, sometimes of antique warrior heroes. Indian rulers adopted red serge jackets for their own forces and retainers as if to capture their magical qualities."[[56]](#cite_note-bayly-84-86-56)

In 1796, under pressure from the company's board of directors in London, the Indian troops were re-organised and reduced during the tenure of [John Shore](/wiki/John_Shore,_1st_Baron_Teignmouth "John Shore, 1st Baron Teignmouth") as Governor-General.[[58]](#cite_note-igi-333-58) However, the closing years of the 18th century saw, with Wellesley's campaigns, a new increase in the army strength. Thus in 1806, at the time of the [Vellore Mutiny](/wiki/Vellore_Mutiny "Vellore Mutiny"), the combined strength of the three presidencies' armies stood at 154,500, making them one of the largest [standing armies](/wiki/Standing_army "Standing army") in the world.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

As the East India Company expanded its territories, it added irregular "local corps", which were not as well trained as the army.[[61]](#cite_note-igi-337-61) In 1846, after the [Second Anglo-Sikh War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War"), a frontier brigade was raised in the [Cis-Sutlej Hill States](/wiki/Cis-Sutlej_states "Cis-Sutlej states") mainly for police work; in addition, in 1849, the "[Punjab Irregular Force](/wiki/Punjab_Irregular_Force "Punjab Irregular Force")" was added on the frontier.[[61]](#cite_note-igi-337-61) Two years later, this force consisted of "3 light field batteries, 5 regiments of cavalry, and 5 of infantry".[[61]](#cite_note-igi-337-61) The following year, "a garrison company was added, ... a sixth infantry regiment (formed from the Sind Camel Corps) in 1853, and one mountain battery in 1856".[[61]](#cite_note-igi-337-61) Similarly, a local force was raised after the annexation of Nagpur in 1854, and the "Oudh Irregular Force" was added after Oudh was annexed in 1856.[[61]](#cite_note-igi-337-61) Earlier, as a result of the treaty of 1800, the [Nizam of Hyderabad](/wiki/Nizam_of_Hyderabad "Nizam of Hyderabad") had begun to maintain a contingent force of 9,000 horse and 6,000-foot which was commanded by Company officers; in 1853, after a new treaty was negotiated, this force was assigned to [Berar](/wiki/Berar_Province "Berar Province") and stopped being a part of the Nizam's army.[[61]](#cite_note-igi-337-61)

In the Indian rebellion of 1857 almost the entire Bengal army, both regular and irregular, revolted.[[62]](#cite_note-igi-338-62) It has been suggested that after the annexation of Oudh by the East India Company in 1856, many sepoys were disquieted both from losing their perquisites, as landed gentry, in the Oudh courts and from the anticipation of any increased land-revenue payments that the annexation might augur.[[63]](#cite_note-brownp88-63) With British victories in wars or with annexation, as the extent of British jurisdiction expanded, the soldiers were now not only expected to serve in less familiar regions (such as in Burma in the [Anglo-Burmese Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Burmese_Wars "Anglo-Burmese Wars") in 1856), but also make do without the "foreign service", remuneration that had previously been their due, and this caused resentment in the ranks.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The Bombay and Madras armies, and the Hyderabad contingent, however, remained loyal. The Punjab Irregular Force not only did not revolt, it played an active role in suppressing the mutiny.[[62]](#cite_note-igi-338-62) The rebellion led to a complete re-organisation of the Indian army in 1858 in the new [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").

### Civil service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Civil service")]

The reforms initiated after 1784 were designed to create an elite civil service where very talented young Britons would spend their entire careers. Advanced training was promoted especially at the [East India Company College](/wiki/East_India_Company_College "East India Company College") (until 1853).[[65]](#cite_note-65) Haileybury emphasised the [Anglican religion](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") and morality and trained students in the classical [Indian languages.](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India") Many students held to [Whiggish](/wiki/Whiggism "Whiggism"), [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism"), and [Utilitarian](/wiki/Utilitarianism "Utilitarianism") convictions of their duty to represent their nation and to modernise India. At most there were about 600 of these men who managed the Raj's customs service, taxes, justice system, and its general administration.[[66]](#cite_note-66)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[67]](#cite_note-67)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The company's original policy was one of "[Orientalism](/wiki/Orientalism "Orientalism")", that is of adjusting to the way of life and customs of the Indian people and not trying to reform them. That changed after 1813, as the forces of reform in the home country, especially evangelical religion, Whiggish political outlook, and Utilitarian philosophy worked together to make the company an agent of Anglicization and modernisation. Christian missionaries became active, but made few converts. The Raj set out to outlaw [sati](/wiki/Sati_(practice) "Sati (practice)") (widow-burning) and [thuggee](/wiki/Thuggee "Thuggee") (ritual banditry) and upgrade the status of women. Schools would be established in which they would teach the English language. The 1830s and 1840s, however, were not times of prosperity: After its heavy spending on the military, the company had little money to engage in large-scale public works projects or modernisation programs.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

## Trade

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Trade")]

Main article: [Economy of India under Company rule](/wiki/Economy_of_India_under_Company_rule "Economy of India under Company rule")

After gaining the right to collect revenue in Bengal in 1765, the Company largely ceased importing [gold and silver](/wiki/Bullion#Bullion "Bullion"), which it had hitherto used to pay for goods shipped back to Britain.[[69]](#cite_note-robb-131-134-69)

Export of bullion to India, by EIC (1708–1810)[[70]](#cite_note-Sivramkrishna2016-70)

In addition, as under Mughal Empire rule, land revenue collected in the Bengal Presidency helped finance the company's wars in other parts of India.[[69]](#cite_note-robb-131-134-69) Consequently, in the period 1760–1800, Bengal's [money supply](/wiki/Money_supply "Money supply") was greatly diminished; furthermore, the closing of some local mints and close supervision of the rest, the fixing of [exchange rates](/wiki/Exchange_rate "Exchange rate"), and the standardisation of [coinage](/wiki/Coin "Coin"), paradoxically, added to the economic downturn.[[69]](#cite_note-robb-131-134-69) During the period, 1780–1860, India changed from being an exporter of processed goods for which it received payment in [bullion](/wiki/Bullion "Bullion"), to being an exporter of [raw materials](/wiki/Raw_materials "Raw materials") and a buyer of [manufactured goods](/wiki/Manufactured_goods "Manufactured goods").[[69]](#cite_note-robb-131-134-69) More specifically, in the 1750s, mostly fine cotton and silk was exported from India to markets in Europe, Asia, and Africa; by the second quarter of the 19th century, raw materials, which chiefly consisted of raw cotton, opium, and indigo, accounted for most of India's exports.[[71]](#cite_note-peers-48-49-71) Also, from the late 18th century British cotton mill industry began to lobby the government to both tax Indian imports and allow them access to markets in India.[[71]](#cite_note-peers-48-49-71) Starting in the 1830s, British textiles began to appear in—and soon to inundate—the Indian markets, with the value of the textile imports growing from £5.2 million 1850 to £18.4 million in 1896.[[72]](#cite_note-72) The [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") too would have a major impact on India's cotton economy: with the outbreak of the war, [American cotton](/wiki/Cotton_production_in_the_United_States "Cotton production in the United States") was no longer available to [British manufacturers](/wiki/Manufacturing_in_the_United_Kingdom "Manufacturing in the United Kingdom"); consequently, demand for Indian cotton soared, and the prices soon quadrupled.[[73]](#cite_note-73) This led many farmers in India to switch to cultivating cotton as a quick cash crop; however, with the end of the war in 1865, the demand plummeted again, creating another downturn in the agricultural economy.[[71]](#cite_note-peers-48-49-71)

At this time, the East India Company's trade with China began to grow as well. In the early 19th century, demand for [Chinese tea](/wiki/Chinese_tea "Chinese tea") had greatly increased in Britain; since the money supply in India was restricted and the company was indisposed to shipping bullion from Britain, it decided upon [opium](/wiki/Opium "Opium"), which had a large underground market in [Qing China](/wiki/Qing_dynasty "Qing dynasty") and which was grown in many parts of India, as the most profitable form of payment.[[74]](#cite_note-peers-49-74) However, since the Chinese authorities had banned the importation and consumption of opium, the Company engaged them in the [First Opium War](/wiki/First_Opium_War "First Opium War"), and at its conclusion, under the [Treaty of Nanjing](/wiki/Treaty_of_Nanjing "Treaty of Nanjing"), gained access to five Chinese ports, [Guangzhou](/wiki/Guangzhou "Guangzhou"), [Xiamen](/wiki/Xiamen "Xiamen"), [Fuzhou](/wiki/Fuzhou "Fuzhou"), Shanghai, and [Ningbo](/wiki/Ningbo "Ningbo"); in addition, Hong Kong was ceded to the British Crown.[[74]](#cite_note-peers-49-74) Towards the end of the second quarter of the 19th century, opium export constituted 40% of India's exports.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Another major, though erratic, export item was [indigo dye](/wiki/Indigo_dye "Indigo dye"), which was extracted from [natural indigo](/wiki/Indigofera_tinctoria "Indigofera tinctoria"), and which came to be grown in Bengal and northern Bihar.[[76]](#cite_note-metcalf-metcalf-76-76) In 1788, the East India Company offered advances to ten British planters to grow indigo; however, since the new (landed) property rights defined in the Permanent Settlement, did not allow them, as Europeans, to buy agricultural land, they had to in turn offer cash advances to local peasants, and sometimes coerce them, to grow the crop.[[77]](#cite_note-bando-125-77) In early 19th century Europe, blue clothing was favoured as a fashion, and blue uniforms were common in the military; consequently, the demand for the dye was high.[[78]](#cite_note-bose-jalal-57-78) The European demand for the dye, however, proved to be unstable, and both creditors and cultivators bore the risk of the market crashes in 1827 and 1847.[[76]](#cite_note-metcalf-metcalf-76-76) The peasant discontent in Bengal eventually led to the *[Indigo rebellion](/wiki/Indigo_rebellion "Indigo rebellion")* in 1859–60 and to the end of indigo production there.[[77]](#cite_note-bando-125-77)[[78]](#cite_note-bose-jalal-57-78) In Bihar, however, indigo production continued well into the 20th century; a centre of indigo production there, [Champaran](/wiki/Champaran "Champaran") district, became an early testing ground, in 1917, for [Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi](/wiki/Mohandas_Karamchand_Gandhi "Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi")'s strategy of [non-violent resistance](/wiki/Non-violent_resistance "Non-violent resistance") against the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj").[[79]](#cite_note-79)

## Justice system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Justice system")]

Until the British gained control of Bengal in later part of 18th century, the system of justice there was presided over by the Nawab of Bengal himself, who, as the chief law officer, *Nawāb Nāzim*, attended to cases qualifying for capital punishment in his headquarters, [Murshidabad](/wiki/Murshidabad "Murshidabad"). His deputy, the *Naib Nāzim*, attended to the slightly less important cases. The ordinary lawsuits belonged to the jurisdiction of a hierarchy of court officials consisting of *faujdārs*, *muhtasils*, and *kotwāls*. In the rural areas, or the *Mofussil*, the *[zamindars](/wiki/Zamindar "Zamindar")*—the rural overlords with the hereditary right to collect rent from peasant farmers—also had the power to administer justice. This they did with little routine oversight, being required to report only their judgments in capital punishment cases to the *Nawāb*.

By the mid-18th century, the East India Company had completed a century and a half presence in India as a trading company, and had a burgeoning presence in the three *presidency* towns of Madras, Bombay, and Calcutta. During this time the successive [Royal Charters](/wiki/Royal_Charter "Royal Charter") had gradually given the East India Company more power to administer justice in these towns. In the charter granted by [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") in 1683, the company was given the power to establish "courts of judicature" in locations of its choice, each court consisting of a lawyer and two merchants. This right was renewed in the subsequent charters granted by [James II](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") and [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England") in 1686 and 1698 respectively. In 1726, however, the Court of Directors of the Company felt that more customary justice was necessary for European residents in the presidency towns, and petitioned the King to establish *Mayor's Courts*. The petition was approved and Mayor's courts, each consisting of a Mayor and nine aldermen, and each having the jurisdiction in lawsuits *between* Europeans, were created in Fort William (Calcutta), Madras, and Bombay. Judgments handed down by a Mayor's Court could be disputed with an appeal to the respective Presidency government and, when the amount disputed was greater than [Rs.](/wiki/Rs. "Rs.") 4,000, with a further appeal to the [King-in-Council](/wiki/King-in-Council "King-in-Council"). In 1753, the Mayor's courts were renewed under a revised [letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent"); in addition, [Courts of Requests](/wiki/Court_of_Requests "Court of Requests") for lawsuits involving amounts less than Rs. 20 were introduced. Both types of courts were regulated by the Court of Directors of the East India Company.

After its victory in the [Battle of Buxar](/wiki/Battle_of_Buxar "Battle of Buxar"), the Company obtained in 1765 the *Diwāni* of Bengal, the right not only to collect revenue, but also to administer civil justice in Bengal. The administration of criminal justice, the *Nizāmat* or *Faujdāri*, however, remained with the *Nawāb*, and for criminal cases the prevailing [Islamic law](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") remained in place. However, the company's new duties associated with the *Diwāni* were leased out to the Indian officials who had formerly performed them. This makeshift arrangement continued—with much accompanying disarray—until 1771, when the Court of Directors of the Company decided to obtain for the company the jurisdiction of both criminal and civil cases.

Soon afterwards Warren Hastings arrived in Calcutta as the first Governor-General of the company's Indian dominions and resolved to overhaul the company's organisation and in particular its judicial affairs. In the interior, or *Mofussil*, *diwāni adālats*, or a [civil courts of first instance](/wiki/Trial_court "Trial court"), were constituted in each district; these courts were presided over by European *Zilā* judges employed by the company, who were assisted in the interpretation of customary Indian law by Hindu *[pandits](/wiki/Pundit_(India) "Pundit (India)")* and Muslim *[qazis](/wiki/Qadi "Qadi")*. For small claims, however, Registrars and Indian commissioners, known as *Sadr Amīns* and *Munsifs*, were appointed. These in their turn were supervised by provincial [civil courts of appeal](/wiki/Appellate_court "Appellate court") constituted for such purpose, each consisting of four British judges. All these were under the authority of the *Sadr Diwāni Adālat*, or the [Chief Civil Court of Appeals](/wiki/Supreme_court "Supreme court"), consisting of the Governor of the Presidency and his Council, assisted by Indian officers.

Similarly for criminal cases, Mofussil *nizāmat adālats*, or Provincial [courts of criminal judicature](/wiki/Criminal_law "Criminal law"), were created in the interior; these again consisted of Indian court officers (*pandits* and *qazis*), who were supervised by officials of the company. Also constituted were [courts of circuit](/wiki/Circuit_court "Circuit court") with [appellate jurisdiction](/wiki/Appellate_jurisdiction "Appellate jurisdiction") in criminal cases, which were usually presided over by the judges of the civil appellate courts. All these too were under a *Sadr Nizāmat Adālat* or a Chief Court of Criminal Appeal.

Around this time the business affairs of the East India Company began to draw increased scrutiny in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Great_Britain "House of Commons of Great Britain"). After receiving a report by a committee, which condemned the Mayor's Courts, [the Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") issued a charter for a new judicial system in the [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency"). The [British Parliament](/wiki/British_Parliament "British Parliament") consequently enacted the [Regulating Act 1773](/wiki/Regulating_Act_1773 "Regulating Act 1773") under which the King-in-Council created a [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_court "Supreme court") in the *Presidency town*, i.e. [Fort William](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta"). The tribunal consisted of one Chief Justice and three [puisne judges](/wiki/Puisne_judge "Puisne judge"); all four judges were to be chosen from [barristers](/wiki/Barrister "Barrister"). The Supreme Court supplanted the Mayor's Court; however, it left the Court of Requests in place. Under the charter, the Supreme Court, moreover, had the authority to exercise all types of jurisdiction in the region of Bengal, Bihar, and Odisha, with the only caveat that in situations where the disputed amount was in excess of Rs. 4,000, their judgment could be appealed to the [Privy Council](/wiki/Privy_Council "Privy Council"). Both the Act and the charter said nothing about the relation between the [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary") (Supreme Court) and the [executive branch](/wiki/Executive_branch "Executive branch") (Governor-General); equally, they were silent on the *Adālats* (both *Diwāni* and *Nizāmat*) created by Warren Hastings just the year before. In the new Supreme Court, the civil and criminal cases alike were interpreted and prosecuted accorded to [English law](/wiki/English_law "English law"); in the *Sadr Adālats*, however, the judges and law-officers had no knowledge of English law, and were required only, by the Governor-General's order, "to proceed according to equity, justice, and good conscience, unless [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu_law "Hindu law") or [Muhammadan law](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") was in point, or some Regulation expressly applied".

There was a good likelihood, therefore, that the Supreme Court and the *Sadr Adālats* would act in opposition to each other and, predictably, many disputes resulted. Hastings' premature attempt to appoint the Chief Justice, [Sir Elijah Impey](/wiki/Elijah_Impey "Elijah Impey"), an old schoolmate from [Winchester](/wiki/Winchester_College "Winchester College"), to the bench of the *Sadr Diwāni Adālat*, only complicated the situation further. The appointment had to be annulled in 1781 by a parliamentary intervention with the enactment of the [Declaration Act 1781](/wiki/Declaration_Act_1781 "Declaration Act 1781"). The act exempted the Executive Branch from the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. It recognised the independent existence of the *Sadr Adālats* and all subsidiary courts of the company. Furthermore, it headed off future legal turf wars by prohibiting the Supreme Court any jurisdiction in matters of revenue (*Diwāni*) or Regulations of the Government enacted by the British Parliament. This state of affairs continued until 1797, when the [East India Act 1797](/w/index.php?title=East_India_Act_1797&action=edit&redlink=1 "East India Act 1797 (page does not exist)") (37 Geo. 3. c. 142) extended the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court to the province of [Benares](/wiki/Benares "Benares") (which had since been added to the company's dominions) and "all places for the time being included in Bengal". With the creation of the [Ceded and Conquered Provinces](/wiki/Ceded_and_Conquered_Provinces "Ceded and Conquered Provinces") in 1805, the jurisdiction would extend as far west as Delhi.

In the other two presidencies, [Madras](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency") and [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency"), a similar course of legal changes unfolded; there, however, the Mayor's Courts were first strengthened to [Recorder's Courts](/wiki/Recorder%27s_Court "Recorder's Court") by adding a legal president to the bench. The Supreme Courts in Madras and Bombay were finally established in 1801 and 1823, respectively. Madras Presidency was also unusual in being the first to rely on village headmen and *[panchāyats](/wiki/Panchayati_raj_in_India "Panchayati raj in India")* for cases involving small claims. This judicial system in the three presidencies was to survive the company's rule, the next major change coming only in 1861.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Education")]

Further information: [History of education in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/History_of_education_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "History of education in the Indian subcontinent")

Education of Indians had become a topic of interest among East India Company officials from the outset of the company's rule in Bengal.[[80]](#cite_note-robb-p137-80) In the last two decades of the 18th century and the first decade of the nineteenth, Company officials pursued a policy of conciliation towards the native culture of its new dominion, especially in relation to education policy.[[80]](#cite_note-robb-p137-80) During the 19th century, the Indian literacy rates were rumoured to be less than half of post independence levels which were 18.33% in 1951. The policy was pursued in the aid of three goals: "to sponsor Indians in their own culture, to advance knowledge of India, and to employ that knowledge in government".[[80]](#cite_note-robb-p137-80)

The first goal was supported by some administrators, such as Warren Hastings, who envisaged the company as the successor of a great Empire, and saw the support of vernacular learning as only befitting that role. In 1781, Hastings founded the *[Madrasa 'Aliya](/wiki/Madrasa_%27Aliya "Madrasa 'Aliya")*, an institution in Calcutta for the study of Arabic and [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") languages, and [Islamic law](/wiki/Islamic_law "Islamic law"). A few decades later a related perspective appeared among the governed population, one that was expressed by the conservative Bengali reformer Radhakanta Deb as the "duty of the Rulers of Countries to preserve and Customs and the religions of their subjects".

The second goal was motivated by the concerns among some Company officials about being seen as foreign rulers. They argued that the company should try to win over its subjects by outdoing the region's previous rulers in the support of indigenous learning. Guided by this belief, the [Benares Sanskrit College](/wiki/Government_Sanskrit_College "Government Sanskrit College") was founded in [Varanasi](/wiki/Varanasi "Varanasi") in 1791 during the administration of Lord Cornwallis. The promotion of knowledge of Asia had attracted scholars as well to the company's service. Earlier, in 1784, the [Asiatick Society](/wiki/Asiatic_Society "Asiatic Society") had been founded in Calcutta by [William Jones](/wiki/William_Jones_(philologist) "William Jones (philologist)"), a [puisne judge](/wiki/Puisne_judge "Puisne judge") in the newly established Supreme Court of Bengal. Soon, Jones was to advance his [famous thesis](/wiki/William_Jones_(philologist)#Scholarly_contributions "William Jones (philologist)") on the common origin of [Indo-European languages](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages").

The third related goal grew out of the philosophy then current among some Company officials that they would themselves become better administrators if they were better versed in the languages and cultures of India. It led in 1800 to the founding of the [College of Fort William](/wiki/College_of_Fort_William "College of Fort William"), in Calcutta by [Lord Wellesley](/wiki/Richard_Wellesley,_1st_Marquess_Wellesley "Richard Wellesley, 1st Marquess Wellesley"), the then Governor-General. The college was later to play an important role both in the development of [modern Indian languages](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India") and in the [Bengal Renaissance](/wiki/Bengal_Renaissance "Bengal Renaissance"). Advocates of these related goals were termed, "[Orientalists](/wiki/Oriental_studies "Oriental studies")". The Orientalist group was led by [Horace Hayman Wilson](/wiki/Horace_Hayman_Wilson "Horace Hayman Wilson"). Many leading Company officials, such as [Thomas Munro](/wiki/Sir_Thomas_Munro,_1st_Baronet "Sir Thomas Munro, 1st Baronet") and [Montstuart Elphinstone](/wiki/Montstuart_Elphinstone "Montstuart Elphinstone"), were influenced by the Orientalist ethos and felt that the company's government in India should be responsive to Indian expectations. The Orientalist ethos would prevail in education policy well into the 1820s, and was reflected in the founding of the [Poona Sanskrit College](/wiki/Deccan_College_(Pune) "Deccan College (Pune)") in [Pune](/wiki/Pune "Pune") in 1821 and the [Calcutta Sanskrit College](/wiki/Sanskrit_College "Sanskrit College") in 1824.

The Orientalists were, however, soon opposed by advocates of an approach that has been termed *[Anglicist](/wiki/Anglicist "Anglicist")*. The Anglicists supported instruction in the English language in order to impart to Indians what they considered modern Western knowledge. Prominent among them were [evangelicals](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") who, after 1813—when the company's territories were opened to [Christian missionaries](/wiki/Mission_(Christian) "Mission (Christian)")—were interested in spreading Christian belief; they also believed in using theology to promote liberal social reform, such as the [abolition of slavery](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abolitionism in the United Kingdom"). Among them was [Charles Grant](/wiki/Charles_Grant_(British_East_India_Company) "Charles Grant (British East India Company)"), the Chairman of the East India Company. Grant supported state-sponsored education in India 20 years before a similar system was set up in Britain. Among Grant's close evangelical friends were [William Wilberforce](/wiki/William_Wilberforce "William Wilberforce"), a prominent [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Abolitionism in the United Kingdom") and member of the British Parliament, and [Sir John Shore](/wiki/John_Shore,_1st_Baron_Teignmouth "John Shore, 1st Baron Teignmouth"), the Governor-General of India from 1793 to 1797. During this period, many Scottish Presbyterian missionaries also supported the British rulers in their efforts to spread English education and established many reputed colleges like [Scottish Church College](/wiki/Scottish_Church_College "Scottish Church College") (1830), [Wilson College](/wiki/Wilson_College,_Mumbai "Wilson College, Mumbai") (1832), [Madras Christian College](/wiki/Madras_Christian_College "Madras Christian College") (1837), and [Elphinstone College](/wiki/Elphinstone_College "Elphinstone College") (1856).

However, the Anglicists also included [utilitarians](/wiki/Utilitarians "Utilitarians"), led by [James Mill](/wiki/James_Mill "James Mill"), who had begun to play an important role in fashioning Company policy. The utilitarians believed in the moral worth of an education that aided the good of society and promoted instruction in *useful knowledge*. Such *useful* instruction to Indians had the added consequence of making them more suitable for the company's burgeoning bureaucracy. By the early 1830s, the Anglicists had the upper hand in devising education policy in India. Many utilitarian ideas were employed in [Thomas Babbington Macaulay](/wiki/Thomas_Babbington_Macaulay "Thomas Babbington Macaulay")'s *[Minute on Indian Education](/wiki/English_Education_Act_1835#Macaulay's_"Minute_Upon_Indian_Education" "English Education Act 1835")* of 1835. The *Minute*, which later aroused great controversy, was to influence education policy in India well into the next century.

Since English was increasingly being employed as the language of instruction, Persian was abolished as the official language of the company's administration and courts by 1837. However, bilingual educations was proving to be popular as well, and some institutions such as the Poona Sanskrit College commenced teaching both Sanskrit and English. Charles Grant's son, [Sir Robert Grant](/wiki/Robert_Grant_(MP) "Robert Grant (MP)"), who in 1834 was appointed Governor of the [Bombay Presidency](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency"), played an influential role in the planning of the first medical college in Bombay, which after his unexpected death was named [Grant Medical College](/wiki/Grant_Medical_College "Grant Medical College") when it was established in 1845. During 1852–1853 some citizens of Bombay sent petitions to the British Parliament in support of both establishing and adequately funding university education in India. The petitions resulted in the [Education Dispatch of 1854](/wiki/Wood%27s_dispatch "Wood's dispatch") sent by [Sir Charles Wood](/wiki/Charles_Wood,_1st_Viscount_Halifax "Charles Wood, 1st Viscount Halifax"), the [President of the Board of Control](/wiki/President_of_the_Board_of_Control "President of the Board of Control") of the East India Company, the chief official on Indian affairs in the British government, to [Lord Dalhousie](/wiki/James_Broun-Ramsay,_1st_Marquess_of_Dalhousie "James Broun-Ramsay, 1st Marquess of Dalhousie"), the then Governor-General of India. The dispatch outlined a broad plan of state-sponsored education for India, which included:[[81]](#cite_note-81)

1. Establishing a Department of Public Instruction in each [presidency or province of British India](/wiki/Provinces_of_British_India "Provinces of British India").
2. Establishing universities modelled on the [University of London](/wiki/University_of_London "University of London") (as primarily examining institutions for students studying in affiliated colleges) in each of the *Presidency towns* (i.e. [Madras](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai"), [Bombay](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"), and Calcutta)
3. Establishing teachers-training schools for all levels of instruction
4. Maintaining existing Government colleges and high-schools and increasing their number when necessary.
5. Vastly increasing vernacular schools for elementary education in villages.
6. Introducing a system of [grants-in-aid](/wiki/Grant-in-aid "Grant-in-aid") for private schools.

The Department of Public Instruction was in place by 1855. In January 1857, the [University of Calcutta](/wiki/University_of_Calcutta "University of Calcutta") was established, followed by the [University of Bombay](/wiki/University_of_Bombay "University of Bombay") in June 1857, and the [University of Madras](/wiki/University_of_Madras "University of Madras") in September 1857. The University of Bombay, for example, consisted of three affiliated institutions: the [Elphinstone Institution](/wiki/Elphinstone_College "Elphinstone College"), the [Grant Medical College](/wiki/Grant_Medical_College "Grant Medical College"), and the [Poona Sanskrit College](/wiki/Deccan_College_(Pune) "Deccan College (Pune)"). The company's administration also founded high-schools *en masse* in the different provinces and presidencies, and the policy was continued during [Crown rule](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") which commenced in 1858. By 1861, 230,000 students were attending public educational institutions in the four provinces (the three Presidencies and [North-Western Provinces](/wiki/North-Western_Provinces "North-Western Provinces")), of whom 200,000 were in primary schools.[[82]](#cite_note-igi-iv-p414-82) Over 5,000 primary schools and 142 secondary schools had been established in these provinces.[[82]](#cite_note-igi-iv-p414-82) Earlier, during the Indian rebellion of 1857, some civilian leaders, such as Khan Bhadur Khan of [Bareilly](/wiki/Bareilly "Bareilly"), had stressed the threat posed to the populace's religions by the new education programmes begun by the company; however, historical statistics have shown that this was not generally the case. For example, in [Etawah](/wiki/Etawah "Etawah") district in the then North-Western Provinces (present-day [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh")), where during the period 1855–1857, nearly 200 primary, middle-, and high-schools had been opened by the company and tax levied on the population, relative calm prevailed and the schools remained open during the rebellion.[[83]](#cite_note-83)
