## Social reform

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Social reform")]

In the first half of the 19th century, the British legislated reforms against what they considered were iniquitous Indian practices. In most cases, the legislation alone was unable to change Indian society sufficiently for it to absorb both the ideal and the ethic underpinning the reform. For example, upper-caste Hindu society in the [Indo-Aryan speaking](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_languages "Indo-Aryan languages") regions of India had long looked askance at the remarriage of widows in order to protect both what it considered was family honour and family property. Even adolescent widows were expected to live a life of austerity and denial.[[84]](#cite_note-Dyson2018-20-84)[[85]](#cite_note-Stein2010-90-85)[[86]](#cite_note-RamusackSievers1999-86) The [Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act, 1856](/wiki/Hindu_Widows%27_Remarriage_Act,_1856 "Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act, 1856"), enacted in the waning years of Company rule, provided legal safeguards against loss of certain forms of inheritance for a remarrying Hindu widow, though not of the inheritance due her from her deceased husband. However, very few widows actually remarried. Some Indian reformers, such as [Raja Ram Mohan Roy](/wiki/Raja_Ram_Mohan_Roy "Raja Ram Mohan Roy"), [Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar](/wiki/Ishwar_Chandra_Vidyasagar "Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar"), even offered money to men who would take widows as brides, but these men often deserted their new wives.

## Post and telegraph

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Post and telegraph")]

### Postal services

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Postal services")]

Further information: [Postal history of India](/wiki/Postal_history_of_India "Postal history of India")

Before 1837, the East India Company's dominions in India had no universal public [postal service](/wiki/Postal_administration "Postal administration"), one that was shared by all regions. Although [courier services](/wiki/Courier_service "Courier service") did exist, connecting the more important towns with their respective seats of provincial government (i.e. the *Presidency towns* of Fort William ([Calcutta](/wiki/Calcutta "Calcutta")), [Fort St. George](/wiki/Fort_St._George "Fort St. George") (Madras), and [Bombay](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay")), private individuals were, upon payment, only sparingly allowed their use. That situation changed in 1837, when, by Act XVII of that year, a public post, run by the company's Government, was established in the company's territory in India. Post offices were established in the principal towns and postmasters appointed. The postmasters of the Presidency towns oversaw a few provincial post offices in addition to being responsible for the main postal services between the provinces. By contrast, the [District collectors](/wiki/District_collector "District collector") (originally, collectors of land-tax) directed the District post offices, including their local postal services. Postal services required payment in cash, to be made in advance, with the amount charged usually varying with weight and distance. For example, the charge of sending a letter from Calcutta to Bombay was one [rupee](/wiki/Rupee "Rupee"); however, that from Calcutta to [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra") was 12 [annas](/wiki/Anna_(monetary_subunit) "Anna (monetary subunit)") (or three-quarter of a rupee) for each [tola](/wiki/Tola_(mass) "Tola (mass)") (three-eighths of an ounce).[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[88]](#cite_note-88)

After the recommendations of the commission appointed in 1850 to evaluate the Indian postal system were received, Act XVII of 1837 was superseded by the Indian Postal Act of 1854. Under its provisions, the entire postal department was headed by a *Director-General*, and the duties of a *Postmaster-General* were set apart from those of a Presidency Postmaster; the former administered the postal system of the larger provinces (such as the [Bombay Presidency](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency") or the [North-Western Provinces](/wiki/North-Western_Provinces "North-Western Provinces")), whereas the latter attended to the less important Provinces (such as [Ajmer-Merwara](/wiki/Ajmer-Merwara "Ajmer-Merwara") and the major Political Agencies such as [Rajputana](/wiki/Rajputana_Agency "Rajputana Agency")). Postage stamps were introduced at this time and the postal rates fixed by weight, dependent no longer also on the distance travelled in the delivery. The lowest inland letter rate was half anna for 1⁄4 tola, followed by one anna for 1⁄2 tola, and 2 annas for a tola, a great reduction from the rates of 17 years before. The [Indian Post Office](/wiki/Indian_Post_Office "Indian Post Office") delivered letters, newspapers, postcards, book packets, and parcels. These deliveries grew steadily in number; by 1861 (three years after the end of Company rule), a total of 889 post offices had been opened, and almost 43 million letters and over four and a half million newspapers were being delivered annually.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

### Telegraphy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Telegraphy")]

Before the advent of [electric telegraphy](/wiki/Electrical_telegraph "Electrical telegraph"), the word "telegraph" had been used for [semaphore](/wiki/Semaphore_line "Semaphore line") signalling. During the period 1820–1830, the East India Company's Government in India seriously considered constructing signalling towers ("telegraph" towers), each a hundred feet high and separated from the next by eight miles, along the entire distance from Calcutta to Bombay. Although such towers were built in Bengal and Bihar, the India-wide semaphore network never took off. By mid-century, electric telegraphy had become viable, and hand signalling obsolete.

[W. B. O'Shaughnessy](/wiki/William_Brooke_O%27Shaughnessy "William Brooke O'Shaughnessy"), a professor of chemistry in the [Calcutta Medical College](/wiki/Medical_College_Kolkata "Medical College Kolkata"), received permission in 1851 to conduct a trial run for a telegraph service from Calcutta to [Diamond Harbour](/wiki/Diamond_Harbour "Diamond Harbour") along the river [Hooghly](/wiki/Hooghly_River "Hooghly River"). Four telegraph offices, mainly for shipping-related business, were also opened along the river that year. The telegraph receiver used in the trial was a [galvanoscope](/wiki/Galvanometer "Galvanometer") of O'Shaughnessy's design and manufactured in India. When the experiment was deemed to be a success a year later, the Governor-General of India, [Lord Dalhousie](/wiki/James_Broun-Ramsay,_1st_Marquess_of_Dalhousie "James Broun-Ramsay, 1st Marquess of Dalhousie"), sought permission from the Court of Directors of the company for the construction of [telegraph lines](/wiki/Telegraph_line "Telegraph line") from "Calcutta to Agra, Agra to Bombay, Agra to Peshawar, and Bombay to Madras, extending in all over 3,050 miles and including forty-one offices". The permission was soon granted; by February 1855 all the proposed telegraph lines had been constructed and were being used to send paid messages. O'Shaughnessy's instrument was used all over India until early 1857, when it was supplanted by the [Morse instrument](/wiki/Morse_code "Morse code"). By 1857, the telegraph network had expanded to 4,555 miles of lines and sixty two offices, and had reached as far as the [hill station](/wiki/Hill_station "Hill station") of [Ootacamund](/wiki/Ootacamund "Ootacamund") in the [Nilgiri Hills](/wiki/Nilgiri_Hills "Nilgiri Hills") and the port of [Calicut](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode") on the southwest coast of India. During the Indian rebellion of 1857, more than seven hundred miles of telegraph lines were destroyed by the rebel forces, mainly in the [North-Western Provinces](/wiki/North-Western_Provinces "North-Western Provinces"). The East India Company was nevertheless able to use the remaining intact lines to warn many outposts of impending disturbances. The political value of the new technology was, thus, driven home to the company, and, in the following year, not only were the destroyed lines rebuilt, but the network was expanded further by 2,000 miles.[[90]](#cite_note-90)

O'Shaughnessy's experimental set-up of 1851–52 consisted of both overhead and underground lines; the latter included underwater ones that crossed two rivers, the [Hooghly](/wiki/Hooghly_river "Hooghly river") and the [Haldi](/wiki/Haldi_River "Haldi River"). The overhead line was constructed by welding uninsulated iron rods, 13+1⁄2 feet long and 3/8 inch wide, end to end. These lines, which weighed 1,250 pounds per mile, were held aloft by fifteen-foot lengths of bamboo, planted into the ground at equal intervals—200 to the mile—and covered with a layer each of coal tar and [pitch](/wiki/Pitch_(resin) "Pitch (resin)") for insulation. The underwater cables had been manufactured in England and consisted of [copper wire](/wiki/Copper_wire "Copper wire") covered with [gutta-percha](/wiki/Gutta-percha "Gutta-percha"). Furthermore, in order to protect the cables from dragging [ship anchors](/wiki/Anchor "Anchor"), the cables were attached to the links of a 7⁄8-inch-thick (22 mm) chain cable. An underwater cable of length 2,070 yards was laid across the Hooghly river at [Diamond Harbour](/wiki/Diamond_Harbour "Diamond Harbour"), and another, 1,400 yards long, was laid across the Haldi at [Kedgeree](/wiki/Khejuri_II#Khijri "Khejuri II").

Work on the long lines from Calcutta to [Peshawar](/wiki/Peshawar "Peshawar") (through Agra), Agra to Bombay, and Bombay to Madras began in 1853. The conducting material chosen for these lines was now lighter, and the support stronger. The wood used for the support consisted of teak, sal, [fir](/wiki/Fir "Fir"), [ironwood](/wiki/Ironwood "Ironwood"), or blackwood (*Terminalia elata*), and was either fashioned into whole posts, or used in attachments to iron *screw-piles* or [masonry](/wiki/Masonry "Masonry") columns. Some sections had uniformly strong support; one such was the 322-mile Bombay-Madras line, which was supported by [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") [obelisks](/wiki/Obelisks "Obelisks") sixteen feet high. Other sections had less secure support, consisting, in some cases, of sections of [toddy palm](/wiki/Borassus_flabellifer "Borassus flabellifer"), insulated with pieces of sal wood fastened to their tops. Some of the conducting wires or rods were insulated, the insulating material being manufactured in either India or England; other stretches of wire remained uninsulated. By 1856, iron tubes had begun to be employed to provide support, and would see increased use in the second half of the 19th century all over India.

The first Telegraph Act for India was Parliament's Act XXXIV of 1854. When the public telegramme service was first set up in 1855, the charge was fixed at one rupee for every sixteen words (including the address) for every 400 miles of transmission. The charges were doubled for telegrammes sent between 6PM and 6AM. These rates would remain fixed until 1882. In the year 1860–61, two years after the end of Company rule, India had 11,093 miles of telegraph lines and 145 telegraph offices. That year telegrams totalling [Rs.](/wiki/Indian_Rupees "Indian Rupees") 500,000 in value were sent by the public, the working expense of the *Indian Telegraph Department* was Rs. 1.4 million, and the [capital expenditure](/wiki/Capital_expenditure "Capital expenditure") until the end of the year totalled Rs. 6.5 million.

## Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Railways")]

Further information: [Rail transport in India § History](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_India#History "Rail transport in India")

The first [inter-city railway](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") service in England, the [Stockton and Darlington Railway](/wiki/Stockton_and_Darlington_Railway "Stockton and Darlington Railway"), had been established in 1825;[[91]](#cite_note-91) in the following decade other inter-city railways were rapidly constructed between cities in England. In 1845, the Court of Directors of the East India Company, forwarded to the Governor-General of India, [Lord Dalhousie](/wiki/James_Broun-Ramsay,_1st_Marquess_of_Dalhousie "James Broun-Ramsay, 1st Marquess of Dalhousie"), a number of applications they had received from private contractors in England for the construction of a wide-ranging railway network in India, and requested a feasibility report. They added that, in their view, the enterprise would be profitable only if large sums of money could be raised for the construction. The Court was concerned that in addition to the usual difficulties encountered in the construction of this new form of transportation, India might present some unique problems, among which they counted floods, tropical storms in coastal areas, damage by "insects and luxuriant tropical vegetation", and the difficulty of finding qualified technicians at a reasonable cost. It was suggested, therefore, that three experimental lines be constructed and their performance evaluated.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

Contracts were awarded in 1849 to the [East Indian Railway Company](/wiki/East_Indian_Railway_Company "East Indian Railway Company") to construct a 120-mile railway from [Howrah](/wiki/Howrah "Howrah")-Calcutta to [Raniganj](/wiki/Raniganj "Raniganj"); to the [Great Indian Peninsular Railway Company](/wiki/Great_Indian_Peninsular_Railway "Great Indian Peninsular Railway") for a service from Bombay to [Kalyan](/wiki/Kalyan,_India "Kalyan, India"), thirty miles away; and to the *Madras Railway Company* for a line from [Madras city](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai") to [Arkonam](/wiki/Arakkonam "Arakkonam"), a distance of some thirty nine miles. Although construction began first, in 1849, on the East Indian Railways line, with an outlay of £1 million, it was the first-leg of the Bombay-Kalyan line—a 21-mile stretch from Bombay to [Thane](/wiki/Thane "Thane")—that, in 1853, was the first to be completed (see picture below).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/IndianRailways1871b.jpg/250px-IndianRailways1871b.jpg)](/wiki/File:IndianRailways1871b.jpg)

Map of the completed and planned railway lines in India in 1871, thirteen years after the end of Company rule.

The feasibility of a train network in India was comprehensively discussed by Lord Dalhousie in his *Railway minute of 1853*. The Governor-General vigorously advocated the quick and widespread introduction of railways in India, pointing to their political, social, and economic advantages. He recommended that a network of *trunk lines* be first constructed connecting the inland regions of each presidency with its chief port as well as each presidency with several others. His recommended trunk lines included the following ones: (i) from Calcutta, in the [Bengal Presidency](/wiki/Bengal_Presidency "Bengal Presidency"), on the eastern coast to [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") in the north-western region of the [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region"), annexed just three years before; (ii) from [Agra](/wiki/Agra "Agra") in north-central India (in, what was still being called [North-Western Provinces](/wiki/North-Western_Provinces "North-Western Provinces")) to [Bombay city](/wiki/Bombay "Bombay") on the western coast; (iii) from Bombay to [Madras city](/wiki/Chennai "Chennai") on the southeastern coast; and (iv) from Madras to the southwestern Malabar coast (see map above). The proposal was soon accepted by the Court of Directors.

During this time work had been proceeding on the experimental lines as well. The first leg of the East Indian Railway line, a [broad gauge](/wiki/Broad_gauge "Broad gauge") railway, from Howrah to [Pandua](/wiki/Pandua,_Hooghly "Pandua, Hooghly"), was opened in 1854 (see picture of locomotive below), and the entire line up to Raniganj would become functional by the time of the Indian rebellion of 1857. The Great Indian Peninsular Railway was permitted to extend its experimental line to [Poona](/wiki/Poona "Poona"). This extension required planning for the steep rise in the *Bor Ghat* valley in the [Western Ghats](/wiki/Western_Ghats "Western Ghats"), a section 15+3⁄4 miles long with an ascent of 1,831 feet. Construction began in 1856 and was completed in 1863, and, in the end, the line required a total of twenty five tunnels and fifteen miles of gradients (inclines) of 1 in 50 or steeper, the most extreme being the *Bor Ghat Incline*, a distance of 1+3⁄4 miles at a gradient of 1 in 37 (see picture above).

Each of the three companies (and later five others that were given contracts in 1859) was a [joint stock](/wiki/Joint_stock "Joint stock") company [domiciled](/wiki/Domicile_(law) "Domicile (law)") in England with its [financial capital](/wiki/Financial_capital "Financial capital") raised in [pounds sterling](/wiki/Pounds_sterling "Pounds sterling"). Each company was guaranteed a 5 per cent return on its capital outlay and, in addition, a share of half the profits. Although the Government of India had no [capital expenditure](/wiki/Capital_expenditure "Capital expenditure") other than the provision of the underlying land free of charge, it had the onus of continuing to provide the 5 percent return in the event of net loss, and soon all anticipation of profits would fall by the wayside as the outlays would mount.

The technology of railway construction was still new and there was no [railway engineering](/wiki/Railway_engineering "Railway engineering") expertise in India; consequently, all engineers had to be brought in from England. These engineers were unfamiliar not only with the language and culture of India, but also with the physical aspect of the land itself and its concomitant engineering requirements. Moreover, never before had such a large and complex construction project been undertaken in India, and no pool of semi-skilled labour was already organised to aid the engineers. The work, therefore, proceeded in fits and starts—many practical trials followed by a final construction that was undertaken with great caution and care—producing an outcome that was later criticised as being "built to a standard which was far in excess of the needs to the time". The Government of India's administrators, moreover, made up in their attention to the fine details of expenditure and management what they lacked in professional expertise. The resulting delays soon led to the appointment of a Committee of the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") in 1857–58 to investigate the matter. However, by the time the Committee concluded that all parties needed to honour the spirit rather than the letter of the contracts, Company rule in India had ended.

Although, railway construction had barely begun in the last years of this rule, its foundations had been laid, and it would proceed apace for much of the next half century. By the turn of the 20th century, India would have over 28,000 miles of railways connecting most interior regions to the ports of Karachi, Bombay, Madras, Calcutta, [Chittagong](/wiki/Chittagong "Chittagong"), and [Rangoon](/wiki/Rangoon "Rangoon"), and together they would constitute the fourth-largest railway network in the world.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

## Canals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Company_rule_in_India&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Canals")]

The first irrigation works undertaken during the East India Company's rule began in 1817. Consisting chiefly of extensions or reinforcements of previous Indian works, these projects were limited to the plains north of Delhi and to the river deltas of the [Madras Presidency](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency").[[95]](#cite_note-stone2002-p13-95)

In plains above Delhi, the mid-14th century [Sultan of Delhi](/wiki/Sultan_of_Delhi "Sultan of Delhi"), [Firoz Shah Tughlaq](/wiki/Firoz_Shah_Tughlaq "Firoz Shah Tughlaq"), had constructed the 150-mile-long *Western Jamna Canal*. Taking off from the right bank of the [Jamna river](/wiki/Yamuna_river "Yamuna river") early in its course, the canal irrigated the Sultan's territories in the [Hissar](/wiki/Hisar_District "Hisar District") region of [Eastern Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"). By the mid-16th century, however, the fine sediment carried by the [Himalayan](/wiki/Himalaya "Himalaya") river had gradually choked the canal. Desilted and reopened several decades later by [Akbar the Great](/wiki/Akbar_the_Great "Akbar the Great"), the *Western Jamna Canal* was itself tapped by Akbar's grandson [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan"), and some of its water was diverted to Delhi. During this time, another canal was cut off the river. The 129-mile *Eastern Jamna Canal* or *Doab Canal*, which took off from the left bank of the Jamna, also high in its course, presented a qualitatively different difficulty. Since it was routed through steeply sloped land, its flow was difficult to control, and it never operated efficiently. With the decline of the Mughal Empire's power in the 18th century, both canals fell into disrepair.[[96]](#cite_note-stone2002-p14-15-96) The Western Jamna Canal was repaired by [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army") engineers, and it reopened in 1820. The Doab Canal was reopened in 1830; its considerable renovation involved raising the embankment by an average height of 9 ft for some 40 miles.[[97]](#cite_note-stone2002-p15-97)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/2b/GangesCanal2.jpg/250px-GangesCanal2.jpg)](/wiki/File:GangesCanal2.jpg)

The Ganges Canal highlighted in red stretching between its headworks off the [Ganges river](/wiki/Ganges_river "Ganges river") in [Hardwar](/wiki/Haridwar_district "Haridwar district") and its confluence with the [Jumna river](/wiki/Yamuna_River "Yamuna River") below [Cawnpore](/wiki/Cawnpore "Cawnpore") (now Kanpur).

Farther west in the [Punjab region](/wiki/Punjab_region "Punjab region"), the 130-mile long *Hasli Canal*, had been constructed by previous rulers.[[95]](#cite_note-stone2002-p13-95) Taking off from the [Ravi river](/wiki/Ravi_river "Ravi river") and supplying water to the cities of [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore") and [Amritsar](/wiki/Amritsar "Amritsar"), this left-bank canal was extended by the British in the *[Bari Doab](/wiki/Bari_Doab "Bari Doab") Canal* works during 1850–1857. The Punjab region, moreover, had much rudimentary irrigation by "inundation canals". Consisting of open cuts along the sides of rivers and unregulated, the inundation canals had been used in both the Punjab and [Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") for many centuries. Many such canals in [Multan](/wiki/Multan "Multan"), [Dera Ghazi Khan](/wiki/Dera_Ghazi_Khan "Dera Ghazi Khan"), and [Muzaffargarh](/wiki/Muzaffargarh "Muzaffargarh") were still working efficiently at the time of the British [annexation of the Punjab](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Sikh_War "Second Anglo-Sikh War") in 1849.[[98]](#cite_note-stone2002-pp13-14-98)

The first new British work—with no Indian antecedents—was the [Ganges Canal](/wiki/Ganges_Canal "Ganges Canal") built between 1842 and 1854.[[99]](#cite_note-stone2002-p16-99)
Contemplated first by Col. [John Russell Colvin](/wiki/John_Russell_Colvin "John Russell Colvin") in 1836, it did not at first elicit much enthusiasm from its eventual architect, Sir [Proby Thomas Cautley](/wiki/Proby_Thomas_Cautley "Proby Thomas Cautley"), who baulked at the idea of cutting a canal through extensive low-lying land to reach the drier upland destination. However, after the [Agra famine of 1837–38](/wiki/Agra_famine_of_1837%E2%80%9338 "Agra famine of 1837–38"), during which the East India Company's administration spent [Rs.](/wiki/Rs. "Rs.") 2,300,000 on famine relief, the idea of a canal became more attractive to the company's Court of Directors.[[100]](#cite_note-stone2002-pp16-17-100) In 1839, the [Governor General of India](/wiki/Governor_General_of_India "Governor General of India"), [Lord Auckland](/wiki/George_Eden,_1st_Earl_of_Auckland "George Eden, 1st Earl of Auckland"), with the Court's assent, granted funds to Cautley for a complete survey of the swath of land that underlay and fringed the projected course of the canal. The Court of Directors, moreover, considerably enlarged the scope of the projected canal, which, in consequence of the severity and geographical extent of the famine, they now deemed to be the entire [Doab](/wiki/Doab "Doab") region.[[100]](#cite_note-stone2002-pp16-17-100)

The enthusiasm, however, proved to be short-lived. Auckland's successor as Governor General, [Lord Ellenborough](/wiki/Edward_Law,_1st_Earl_of_Ellenborough "Edward Law, 1st Earl of Ellenborough"), appeared less receptive to large-scale public works, and during his tenure withheld major funds for the project.[[101]](#cite_note-stone2002-p17-101) Only in 1844, when a new Governor-General, [Lord Hardinge](/wiki/Henry_Hardinge,_1st_Viscount_Hardinge "Henry Hardinge, 1st Viscount Hardinge"), was appointed, did official enthusiasm and funds return to the Ganges canal project. Although the intervening impasse had seemingly affected Cautely's health and required him to return to Britain in 1845 for recuperation, his European sojourn allowed him to study contemporary hydraulic works in Great Britain and Italy. By the time of his return to India even more supportive men were at the helm, both in the [North-Western Provinces](/wiki/North-Western_Provinces "North-Western Provinces"), with [James Thomason](/wiki/James_Thomason_(British_colonial_governor) "James Thomason (British colonial governor)") as Lt. Governor, and in [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") with [Lord Dalhousie](/wiki/James_Broun-Ramsay,_1st_Marquess_of_Dalhousie "James Broun-Ramsay, 1st Marquess of Dalhousie") as Governor-General.[[102]](#cite_note-stone2002-p17-18-102) Canal construction, under Cautley's supervision, now went into high activity. A 350-mile long canal, with another 300 miles of branch lines, eventually stretched between the headworks in [Hardwar](/wiki/Haridwar_district "Haridwar district") and—after splitting into two branches at Nanau near [Aligarh](/wiki/Aligarh "Aligarh")—the confluence with the Ganges at [Cawnpore](/wiki/Cawnpore "Cawnpore") (now Kanpur) and with the Jumna (now [Yamuna](/wiki/Yamuna "Yamuna")) [mainstem](/wiki/Mainstem_(hydrology) "Mainstem (hydrology)") at [Etawah](/wiki/Etawah "Etawah"). The Ganges Canal, which required a total capital outlay of £2.15 million, was officially opened in 1854 and became fully operational in 1857.[[103]](#cite_note-stone2002-18-103) According to historian Ian Stone:

> It was the largest canal ever attempted in the world, five times greater in its length than all the main irrigating lines of [Lombardy](/wiki/Lombardy "Lombardy") and Egypt put together, and longer by a third than even the largest USA navigation canal, the [Pennsylvania Canal](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Canal "Pennsylvania Canal").[[104]](#cite_note-stone2002-p18-104)

84. **[^](#cite_ref-Dyson2018-20_84-0)** Dyson, Tim (2018), [*A Population History of India: From the First Modern People to the Present Day*](https://books.google.com/books?id=3TRtDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA20), Oxford University Press, p. 20, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-19-882905-8](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-19-882905-8 "Special:BookSources/978-0-19-882905-8"), "Therefore, by the time of the Mauryan Empire the position of women in mainstream Indo-Aryan society seems to have deteriorated. Customs such as child marriage and dowry were becoming entrenched; and a young women's purpose in life was to provide sons for the male lineage into which she married. To quote the Arthashāstra: 'wives are there for having sons'. Practices such as female infanticide and the neglect of young girls were also developing at this time. Further, due to the increasingly hierarchical nature of the society, marriage was becoming a mere institution for childbearing and the formalization of relationships between groups. In turn, this may have contributed to the growth of increasingly instrumental attitudes towards women and girls (who moved home at marriage). It is important to note that, in all likelihood, these developments did not affect people living in large parts of the subcontinent—such as those in the south, and tribal communities inhabiting the forested hill and plateau areas of central and eastern India. That said, these deleterious features have continued to blight Indo-Aryan speaking areas of the subcontinent until the present day"

Sievers (ed.), *Women in Asia: Restoring Women to History*, Indiana University Press, pp. 27–29, [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-253-21267-7](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-253-21267-7 "Special:BookSources/0-253-21267-7"), "The legal rights, as well as the ideal images, of women were increasingly circumscribed during the Gupta era. The Laws of Manu, compiled from about 200 to 400 C.E., came to be the most prominent evidence that this era was not necessarily a golden age for women. Through a combination of legal injunctions and moral prescriptions, women were firmly tied to the patriarchal family, ... Thus the Laws of Manu severely reduced the property rights of women, recommended a significant difference in ages between husband and wife and the relatively early marriage of women, and banned widow remarriage. Manu's preoccupation with chastity reflected possibly a growing concern for the maintenance of inheritance rights in the male line, a fear of women undermining the increasingly rigid caste divisions, and a growing emphasis on male asceticism as a higher spiritual calling."
